The adoption of Enterprise AI has slowed down active deployment in 42% of large companies, while 40% remain stuck in exploration phases – a bottleneck that costs companies a competitive advantage as early adopters progress with accelerated investments.
The 42% of companies using AI double: 59% have accelerated their AI investments over the last 24 months, suggesting that they have enough value to justify expansion.
Geographical differences are strong: India (59%) and the United Arab Emirates (58%) are leading, while France (26%) and Spain (28%) lag behind.
Financial services lead to the introduction of industry at around 50% compared to 37% in telecommunications.
The primary deployment uses IT automation center (33%), security and threat detection (26%) and AI governance (25%).
Three barriers prevent the remaining 40% from moving beyond the experiment: limited AI knowledge and know-how (33%), data complexity (25%) and ethical concerns (23%).
The skill gap manifests itself in concrete terms: 20% of organisations do not have qualified employees to use AI tools, and 16% cannot adjust to close this gap.
Especially for generative AI, data protection concerns concern 57% of non-adopters and confidence problems 43%.

Organisations recognise the importance of responsible AI practices – 85% agree that consumers prefer transparent AI ethics – yet less than half of the companies have implemented distortion reduction (27%), data origin tracking (37%), or ethical AI policies (44%).
Only 34% train employees to work alongside AI tools.

In addition to AI implementation, organisations should prioritise workforce reskilling programmes, as the skills gap is the only major barrier and only one third of companies are currently investing in training.
This restriction would unlock both the availability capability and trusted AI practices that account for 85% of IT professionals in consumer demand.
